abstract: The crystal structure of Cd3As2, a high-mobility semimetal known for hosting electronic Dirac particles, has been reinvestigated using single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Our analysis reveals that the material is centrosymmetric, contrary to previous reports of a noncentrosymmetric structure. Cd3As2 crystallizes in the tetragonal space group $I4_1/acd$ with unit cell parameters $a = 12.633(3)$ and $c = 25.427(7)$ Å, forming a distorted superstructure of the antifluorite ($M_2X$) type. This architecture consists of distorted $Cd_6\square_2$ cubes (where $\square$ represents a vacancy) arranged in parallel columns that stack with opposing chirality. Electronic structure calculations based on this centrosymmetric model demonstrate that the presence of inversion symmetry prevents spin splitting; consequently, all bands remain spin-degenerate. This results in a four-fold degenerate bulk Dirac point at the Fermi energy along the $\Gamma-Z$ direction in the Brillouin zone, establishing Cd3As2 as a three-dimensional electronic analogue of graphene. Furthermore, scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) identifies a $2 \times 2$ surface reconstruction on the $(112)$ cleavage plane, while morphological analysis confirms that needle-like crystals grow along the $[110]$ axis.
